title: pd-fleet.yml
description: Declarative configuration for a Port Daddy background agent fleet. Authoritative source: lib/fleet-engine.ts (FleetYamlRoot).

name.description: Fleet name. MUST equal basename(projectDir) so the post-commit hook's project:<basename>:<hash>:git:committed channel matches what fleet-channels.ts subscribes on. Renaming silently breaks every git:committed-triggered agent.

harbor.description: Semantic identity of the shared harbor for this fleet. Template variables: {project}.

channels.description: Pub/sub channel registry. Used by validateTopology to catch dead-end channels.

limits.description: Fleet-wide spawn quotas enforced at trigger time by FleetRunner.

schedule.description: Cron expression. Mutually compatible with trigger / triggerTuple — agent fires on any of them.

trigger.description: Channel name. Agent fires on every message published to this channel.

triggerTuple.description: Linda-style tuple pattern. Agent fires when a tuple matching this shape lands in the harbor's tuple space.

model.description: Pin the model whenever the backend supports it for predictable cost.

prompt.description: Prompt or shell command (depending on backend). For backend=custom, this IS the command; for LLM backends, this is the user message.

singleton.description: If true, fleet refuses to spawn a second instance while one is running.

on_success.description: Action on exit code 0. Currently supports `publish <channel>`.

identity.description: Semantic identity for this agent's PD registration. Template variables: {project}.

allowedTools.description: Claude-CLI-style tool allowlist. Comma-separated. Bash() and other parametrized tools are supported.

exec.description: Shell command to run when message arrives.

condition.description: Optional shell condition; exec only runs when this exits 0.

consumers.description: Names of agents/watchers expected to subscribe. Topology validator warns if no consumer is registered.

external_producer.description: If true or a label, the topology validator allows the channel to have no in-fleet producer (it's published from outside, e.g. by the post-commit hook).
